title: HTTP/2 Frames, Streams, HPACK, Flow Control, and Go Boundaries · Failure Repair Bay
summary: An incident engineer receives a plausible but defective HTTP/2 connection, stream, frame, and compression model; retain the raw transcript, isolate the first divergent state, and preserve a regression corpus entry. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in HTTP/2 connection, stream, frame, and compression model and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence de…
objectives: Trace connection preface, SETTINGS exchange and acknowledgement, frame header, payload length, type, flags, stream identifier, and connection errors.
Apply idle, open, half-closed, closed, reserved, reset, and connection shutdown transitions independently per stream.
Map pseudo-fields and regular fields, enforce ordering and connection-field rules, bound HPACK tables and header lists, and avoid indexing secrets.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug H2 Preface Settings Frames
steps.instruction:
debug case — A debug protocol team handles case 7486 in a multi-tenant intake service near its connection and memory ceilings. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine HTTP/2 frame, stream, flow-control, and header-list bounds; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Trace connection preface, SETTINGS exchange and acknowledgement, frame header, payload length, type, flags, stream identifier, and connection errors.

Inspect the H2 Preface Settings Frames trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

Evidence target: Inspect the failure record · Debug H2 Preface Settings Frames.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for trace connection preface, settings exchange and acknowledgement, frame header, payload length, type, flags, stream identifier, and connection errors. For Inspect the failure record · Debug H2 Preface Settings Frames, keep H2 Preface Settings Frames connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: httpproto-h2-go-transfer-boundary-csiys6" that uses case 4246 to combine HTTP/2 frame, stream, flow-control, and header-list bounds. Browser code must not open sockets or listeners, negotiate TLS, contact networks or proxies, capture packets, read host state, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the named Go 1.26 protocol transfer gates.

steps.title: Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary
steps.instruction:
Write the debug evidence note for H2 Go Transfer Boundary: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A debug protocol team handles case 7494 in a regional status service with slow assistive and low-bandwidth clients. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine HTTP/2 frame, stream, flow-control, and header-list bounds; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use Go 1.26 net/http and current HTTP/2 implementation for production while limiting custom work to models, frame labs, tests, and authorized disposable environments.

Evidence target: Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary, keep H2 Go Transfer Boundary connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use Go 1.26 net/http and current HTTP/2 implementation for production while limiting custom work to models, frame labs, tests, and authorized disposable environments.

hints: For Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary, name the types, ownership, and stop path, H2 Go Transfer Boundary precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A debug protocol team handles case 7494 in a regional status service with slow assistive and low-bandwidth clients. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine HTTP/2 frame, stream, flow-control, and header-list bounds; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use Go 1.26 net/http and current HTTP/2 implementation for production while limiting custom work to models, frame labs, tests, and authorized disposable environments.

For Publish the incident record · Debug H2 Go Transfer Boundary, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A training frame decoder should replace the maintained Go HTTP/2 stack in production.
